concept.headline: Julia comprehensions build arrays from expressions over a range or iterable, with optional filtering. [expr for x in iter if cond] is the core form.
concept.explanation: Array comprehensions are a concise syntax for building arrays by transforming and filtering elements. [x^2 for x in 1:5] creates [1,4,9,16,25]. An optional if clause filters: [x^2 for x in 1:10 if x%2==0] gives squares of even numbers only. Generator expressions (without brackets) are lazy and memory-efficient. Dict and Set comprehensions follow the same pattern.
concept.syntax:
# Basic comprehension
[x^2 for x in 1:5]          # [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

# With filter
[x^2 for x in 1:10 if x%2==0]  # [4, 16, 36, 64, 100]

# Over an array
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
[n * 2 for n in nums]      # [6, 2, 8, 2, 10]

# Generator (lazy, no allocation)
sum(x^2 for x in 1:100)

# Nested comprehension (2D)
[(i,j) for i in 1:2, j in 1:2]  # matrix form
keyPoints: [expr for var in iter] — basic form, returns a Vector
[expr for var in iter if cond] — filtered form
(expr for var in iter) — generator: lazy, no intermediate array
Nest multiple 'for' clauses for multi-dimensional comprehensions

demo.intro: Watch array comprehensions build lists from expressions and filtered conditions.
demo.code:
squares = [x^2 for x in 1:5]
println(squares)

even_squares = [x^2 for x in 1:10 if x%2 == 0]
println(even_squares)

words = ["hello", "world", "julia"]
upper_words = [uppercase(w) for w in words]
println(upper_words)

total = sum(x^2 for x in 1:4)
println(total)

explanation.what: [x^2 for x in 1:5] — computes x² for each x in the range 1,2,3,4,5
explanation.why: Comprehension iterates x over 1:5 and applies x^2 to each value
explanation.misconception: This is equivalent to: result=[]; for x in 1:5; push!(result, x^2); end — but far more concise. The comprehension also communicates INTENT (transform) rather than process (append).
The range 1:5 is inclusive — x takes values 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 (five values, five results)

explanation.what: [x^2 for x in 1:10 if x%2==0] — squares of even numbers 2,4,6,8,10
explanation.why: The if clause filters: only x where x%2==0 (even) are processed
explanation.intent: Show filtered comprehension with if clause
explanation.misconception: The if in a comprehension is a FILTER — it selects which elements from the range get included. It is not an if-else — there is no 'else' clause in a comprehension filter.

explanation.why: Only x=2,4,6,8,10 passed the x%2==0 filter
explanation.misconception: Odd values 1,3,5,7,9 are completely excluded from the result — the comprehension never applies x^2 to them

explanation.what: [uppercase(w) for w in words] — applies uppercase() to each word
explanation.why: Comprehensions work over any iterable, not just ranges
explanation.intent: Show comprehension over an existing array with a function call
explanation.misconception: Comprehensions can use any expression — function calls, arithmetic, string operations — as long as the expression is valid for each element type

explanation.why: uppercase() applied to each String element
explanation.misconception: The original words array is unchanged — comprehensions return a NEW array

explanation.what: sum(x^2 for x in 1:4) — generator expression passed to sum()
explanation.why: Generator (without []) produces values lazily — no intermediate array allocated
explanation.intent: Show generator expression as a memory-efficient alternative to comprehension
explanation.misconception: sum(x^2 for x in 1:4) is more memory-efficient than sum([x^2 for x in 1:4]) — the generator never creates the intermediate array. For aggregations (sum, maximum, etc.), always prefer generators over comprehensions.
A generator cannot be indexed like an array — g = (x^2 for x in 1:4); g[1] throws an error. To index, wrap in collect() or use a comprehension [].

prediction.question:
What is the output?

result = [x for x in 1:10 if x % 3 == 0]
println(length(result))
prediction.explanation: x % 3 == 0 selects multiples of 3 in 1:10: [3, 6, 9] — that's 3 elements. length([3,6,9]) = 3.

explanation.afterDemo: Comprehensions are a hallmark of Julia's expressiveness for data transformation. They replace verbose for-push! loops with a single readable line. The if clause replaces an inner if-block. For aggregation (sum, maximum, any, all) use generators instead of comprehensions — they avoid allocating the intermediate array entirely, which matters for large inputs.
keyTakeaways: [expr for x in iter] — basic transform. [expr for x in iter if cond] — filtered.
(expr for x in iter) — generator: lazy, no memory allocation.
Use generators with sum/maximum/any/all to avoid intermediate arrays.

practice.prompt: Create a list of all numbers from 1 to 20 that are divisible by both 2 and 3. Print the list.
practice.solutionCode:
result = [x for x in 1:20 if x % 2 == 0 && x % 3 == 0]
println(result)
practice.hint: Use x % 2 == 0 && x % 3 == 0 as the filter condition.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! 6, 12, 18 are divisible by both 2 and 3 in 1:20.
retryHints: Divisible by 6 is equivalent to divisible by both 2 and 3

summary.title: Comprehensions — Complete
takeaways: [expr for x in iter] builds an array. Add if cond to filter.
Generator (expr for x in iter) is lazy — no intermediate array.
Use generators with sum/maximum/any/all for memory efficiency on large inputs.
